stem: The integral test compares the log-type series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{[[C]]}{n(\ln n)^{[[p]]}} \) to the improper integral \( \displaystyle\int_{e^{[[k]]}}^{\infty} \frac{[[C]]}{x(\ln x)^{[[p]]}}\,dx \). Since \( p=[[p]]>1 \), substituting \( u=\ln x \) turns this into a convergent p-integral. Compute the value of the integral.
solution: Substitute \( u=\ln x \), \( du = dx/x \); the lower bound \( x=e^{[[k]]} \) becomes \( u=[[k]] \), so the integral becomes \( \displaystyle\int_{[[k]]}^{\infty} [[C]]u^{-[[p]]}\,du = \dfrac{[[C]]}{[[k]]^{[[p]]-1}([[p]]-1)} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This divides by \( p \) instead of \( p-1 \), forgetting the antiderivative shifts the exponent by one.
This multiplies by \( p-1 \) instead of dividing, inverting the correct relationship.
This raises \( k \) to the \( p \) power instead of \( p-1 \), an off-by-one error from the substitution.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This drops the factor of \( k^{p-1} \) entirely, as if the substitution's lower bound were 1.

stem: For the log-type series \( \displaystyle\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n(\ln n)^{[[p]]}} \), the integral test requires evaluating \( \displaystyle\int_{e^{[[k]]}}^{\infty} \frac{dx}{x(\ln x)^{[[p]]}} \) as the limit \( \displaystyle\lim_{t\to\infty} F(t) \), where \( F(t) = \dfrac{(\ln t)^{1-[[p]]} - [[k]]^{1-[[p]]}}{1-[[p]]} \) is the antiderivative evaluated between the bounds. Compute this limit.
distractor_rationales: This mishandles the sign when taking the limit, effectively treating \( (\ln t)^{1-p} \) as if it did not vanish.
This divides by \( p \) instead of \( p-1 \) and drops the exponent adjustment.
This raises \( k \) to the \( p \) power instead of \( p-1 \), an off-by-one exponent error.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign.
